children.value: The seven primary models constitute the confirmatory inferential pipeline. Each is a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) fit on the trial-level dataset (with the exception of M5, which uses the episode-level dataset). All seven share the same three-tier model-fitting fallback ladder, the same ordered-condition contrast coding, and the same circular-data conventions, all documented in

How the Models map onto the Cognitive Process

children.label: How the Models map onto the Cognitive Process

children.alt: How the seven models map onto the cognitive process.

children.value: How the seven models map onto the cognitive process.
Conceptual Map of the Behavioral Signatures

children.label: Conceptual Map of the Behavioral Signatures

children.value: The primary models capture distinct but complementary behavioral signatures. It is crucial to distinguish between where a participant places their net, how spread out their allocation is, and how much they shift from their previous belief.

children.alt: Visual distinction between the primary behavioral signatures. Left (M1/M2): Accuracy refers to the distance of the net placement relative to the true target. Right (M3): Precision (circular variance) is equal to Dispersion and captures the spread of the allocation - placing nets in a tight cluster implies high certainty (A), while spreading them out implies uncertainty or hedging (B).

children.value: Visual distinction between the primary behavioral signatures.
Accuracy refers to the distance of the net placement relative to the true target.

Precision (circular variance) is equal to Dispersion and captures the spread of the allocation - placing nets in a tight cluster implies high certainty (A), while spreading them out implies uncertainty or hedging (B).
Belief-shift captures the trial-to-trial movement of the allocation centroid, measuring reactivity to new evidence or surprise.
Implementation details - a common predictor structure

children.label: Implementation details - a common predictor structure

children.value: To make cross-model comparison clean, the following predictors appear in (almost) every model:
represents the orthogonal-polynomial decomposition of the ordered condition. The headline test in every model is on
serves as a non-monotonicity control. See
for the contrast values and interpretation.
is the within-episode normalised position, in models concerning within-episode dynamics (M1, M3, M6, M7).
is the per-participant centred episode index, in all models that span the session.
is the centred episode length, included as a covariate to absorb the effect of jittered episode duration.
are post-event indicators, included where the post-event response is theoretically interesting.
Random-effect structure varies by model and is documented per-model below.
Before every model fit, the pipeline runs two routine diagnostic checks on the predictor structure.
For each fitted model, the pipeline computes the VIF for every fixed-effect predictor (after dropping the intercept). VIF for predictor

children.value: VIFs operate on the fixed-effect design matrix only and do not account for the random-effect structure. They are most useful for detecting collinearity among chosen predictors (e.g., redundant covariates, near-perfect interactions).

Before the formal models, the pipeline produces a layer of raw descriptive markers that are interpretable without statistical machinery. The general principle is “raw effects → GLMMs → cognitive models” - to start with what anyone can read directly off the data, and then move to abstraction. Three sub-modules are run.

children.alt: The hierarchical principle of the behavioral data analysis framework.

children.value: The hierarchical principle of the behavioral data analysis framework.

In addition to the headline coefficients, the model is augmented with a planned-comparison Wald test of the

, conditional on the L and Q contrast values at each of the three condition levels. The logic is that a participant who correctly discriminates structural change from noise should react more strongly to transitions (which signal a genuine state change) than to oddballs (which signal noise). This is what the differential captures directly, and its modulation by condition is potentially theoretically informative beyond what either marginal coefficient delivers alone.

A practical note on random-effect over-parameterisation

children.label: A practical note on random-effect over-parameterisation

children.value: In small-N data, the three-component random-effect structure (intercept + two slopes) is over-parameterised, with strong induced collinearity between the random intercept and slopes (because participants who shift more on average also tend to shift more after specific events). When the random-effect covariance is not estimable, the fallback ladder progresses to a simpler structure or to cluster-robust OLS. The model-fitting tier actually used is recorded in

children.html: c2c_2c2​

children.label: M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)

children.value: M1 through M4 measure responses to observed outcomes. M5 is the only model that tests inferential generalization to a never-yet-observed cue (c2). It captures whether the participant has learned the latent binary structure of the environment, allowing them to place the c2 nets correctly before any c2 evidence has appeared.

With the random slope, the BLUPs basically become per-participant “evidence-dependence” scores, where a slope near zero = immediate generaliser; large negative slope = evidence-driven generaliser.

Why control for the cue-dissociation pattern seperately?
To test whether participants are doing cue-dissociation at all, separate from how-close-to-target they are.

Why M5b is more informative than M5 regarding the cue dissociation rule

children.label: Why M5b is more informative than M5 regarding the cue dissociation rule

children.html: ∣Ie∣\lvert I_e \rvert∣Ie​∣

children.value: confounds three things: (i) genuine cross-cue inference, (ii) placement noise, and (iii) cue confusion (the placement might happen to be near the new

children.html: c2c_2c2​

children.value: target by virtue of being near the old

children.html: c1c_1c1​

children.value:
target). M5b isolates (i) cleanly by reducing the DV to a binary comparison that is invariant to (ii) and that explicitly contrasts (i) against (iii).
However, M5 and M5b are still formally complementary because M5b tests
cross-cue inference is happening at all while M5 tests
it is when it is happening.
Why a seperate classification in rule-appliers vs naive participants?

Because we want to understand whether participants identify the rules even if we do not tell them what (exactly) is going on (condition 1 and 2) and how long it takes them to do so. Furthermore, also participants that are informed about the rules (condition 3) might forget, misunderstand, discard or simply ignore the rules.

The cue-dissociation analyses (M5b plus the floor diagnostic) reveal substantial inter-individual heterogeneity in how participants engage rule 2. To make this heterogeneity tractable, the pipeline includes a per-participant classification step that sorts participants into rule-application classes based on their cue-dissociation behaviour.
A participant’s behaviour is summarised by their per-episode cue-dissociation rate, computed separately for two episode subsets:
: episodes 1-3 (the period in which a participant could still be constructing the structural model from data).
: episodes 4-8 (after the participant has had time to learn).

children.value: The 0.6 threshold is above chance (random placement yields ~50% cue-dissociation by construction); the 0.5 floor for non-applier ensures the participant is not just near chance.
For each participant in the immediate or late applier class, the

is the smallest episode index at which a 2-episode moving window of cue-dissociation crosses 0.6. For immediate appliers, this is 1 by definition; for late appliers, it is the mid-session episode at which the rule is first reliably applied. The distribution of discovery episodes by condition is a temporal signature of when participants figure out (or apply) the structural rule, which is itself theoretically interesting (and connects to mental-health-linked individual differences as a downstream analysis; follows soon).

children.alt: (A) class proportions by condition; (B) within-session evolution of cue-dissociation rate by class; (C) discovery episode distribution by condition; (D) two-rule space showing each participant’s rule 1 marker vs rule 2 cue-dissociation rate, coloured by class and shaped by condition, visualizes whether rule 1 (post-transition belief shift) and rule 2 (cue dissociation) are aspects of a single “rule-knowledge” dimension or genuinely independent traits.

Because we want to check whether participants adjust their net placement to the new latent state even if they are still in the old one. This would be a signature of latent state inference.

A question raised during pilot analysis was whether some participants (mainly condition 3) anticipate transitions before they occur, by drifting their placements toward the new expected target in the last few trials of the old episode.
routine produces a two-panel figure on the 5 trials immediately preceding each transition.
Panel A - Anticipatory drift toward the next target

: signed angular displacement from the old target, projected onto the direction of the upcoming shift. Positive values mean placements drift toward the new target before it is revealed. Different conditions are plotted as separate trajectories. If condition 3 anticipates, this curve should be positive and rising as the transition approaches; if not, it should be flat at zero.

children.html: ∣et∣\lvert e_t \rvert∣et​∣

children.value: over the same 5-trial window. If condition 3 participants anticipate, pre-transition error should rise in C3 as the transition approaches (because they are drifting away from the old target).

At the current N, the results do not support the idea that participants anticipate the latent state transitions (C3 has lower pre-transition error throughout the window but does not show systematic drift toward the next target). However, this analysis will also be applied to the rule-appliers (that will become informative at greater N though) based on the observations described in the sections above.
Because this provides a control mechanism for the interpretation of the M2 effects.
M2 measures post-transition recovery using raw

children.html: ∣et∣\lvert e_t \rvert∣et​∣

children.value: , which conflates two things: how much was the participant
by the transition, and how good was their placement
the transition. A participant who was tightly placed before the transition is necessarily more perturbed by it (because the new target is

children.html: ∼120°\sim 120°∼120°

children.value: away from their old position), while a participant whose placement was already noisy will appear less perturbed.
To control for this, the pipeline includes a baseline-normalised recovery visualisation.
For each transition, the pre-transition baseline is computed as the mean

children.html: ∣et∣\lvert e_t \rvert∣et​∣

children.value: over the 3 trials immediately before the transition (in the old episode). The recovery trajectory

children.html: ∣et∣/baseline\lvert e_t \rvert / \text{baseline}∣et​∣/baseline

children.value: is then plotted over the 5 trials following the transition.

A ratio of 1.0 means current error equals the pre-transition baseline; a ratio of 2.0 means error has doubled; a ratio below 1.0 means error is even smaller than baseline (which can happen after a successful reorientation if the participants new-state placement is more accurate than their old-state one).

includes to panels showing (A) the recovery ratio trajectory by condition, and (B) the per-participant “peak ratio” (= the largest perturbation each participant experienced relative to their baseline) by condition. Panel B is thus a condition-level summary of

This diagnostic clarifies whether condition differences in post-transition recovery are about speed of reorientation (which should manifest as condition differences in the ratio trajectory) or about baseline placement quality (which should show no condition difference in the ratio after the first 1-2 trials).

The fit method actually used is recorded in
The Bayesian-VB fit is generally preferable here because it produces participant-level random effects analogous to the BLUPs from M1–M6, allowing M7 to contribute to the
analysis. GEE estimates are population-averaged and do not yield per-participant random effects.
Early-phase companion analyses (M1_early, M3_early, M4_early, M7_early)

children.label: Early-phase companion analyses (M1_early, M3_early, M4_early, M7_early)

children.value: We assume that the first ~15-30 trials of each participant’s session might be qualitatively different from the rest of the session for several specific theoretical reasons. For condition 3 participants (rules explained), the early trials are simply the first chance to

the generative model they entered the experiment with - to data that, from their perspective, has the structure they were told it has. For conditions 1 and 2 (basic / rules-mentioned), the same trials are when participants are

their generative model from scratch, e.g. they don’t yet know there are two cues with different distributions, they don’t yet know about the alternating latent state, they don’t yet know which trials are noise to be discounted.
The strongest theoretical prediction of the instruction manipulation is therefore not (only) that condition 3 participants are better on average over the whole session,- it is that they are better

, because they enter with a model that condition 1/2 participants must construct from data. By the end of the session, the conditions may have (partially) converged. That is, condition 1 having figured out the structure from evidence, condition 3 having applied it all along. The condition main effects in the full-session models are then a mixture of “early differences where the manipulation matters” and “late convergence where it does not”, which might partially dilute the manipulation signature (even if interaction effects with the trial/episode index are included, the effect decreases over the experiment as soon as condition 1/2 participants catch up). And the early-phase companion analyses target this head-on by restricting the sample to the early trials and using
(centred within the early-phase sample) as a learning-curve predictor.

) whcih covers episode 0 only. This is the cleanest “initial model identification” sample since no episode transition to confound interpretation (thus, any belief shifts within this window are pure within-state learning).

) covers the first 25 trials per participant (but this is configurable), which captures both initial model identification and the first latent environmental state transition, which is the moment where the binary-state structure first becomes observable.
The companion models share the same learning-curve predictor structure:

DV ~ condition_L + condition_Q
     + trial_index_c
     + condition_L : trial_index_c
     + condition_Q : trial_index_c
with random intercept and (when identifiable) random slope on
, not the full session. A coefficient on
therefore represents per-trial change in the DV over the early-phase window specifically.
) and their interactions are dropped from the early-phase specifications because they are either constant or uninformative within the restricted sample. The lag-1 event indicators (
) are retained in M4_early because the event-driven belief shifts are still substantively interesting in the early window.
. Tests the most direct prediction: do condition 3 participants have lower error early, and do the conditions converge over the early trials?
. Tests whether condition 3 participants concentrate their nets immediately (low circ_var) vs gradual concentration in conditions 1/2.
log(Δ_t + ε) ~ condition × trial_index_c + oddball_lag1 + transition_lag1 + omission_lag1
. Tests belief-shift magnitude in the early phase, where conditions 1/2 should show larger updates because their generative model is still being constructed.

condition effect across the restricted window. Comparing this to the corresponding full-session coefficient is the first test of whether the manipulation is qualitatively early-phase (a substantially larger
in the early-phase model than the full-session model is direct evidence of “dilution” by late-session convergence).
is the quantitative test of convergence. A
coefficient (on a higher-error DV) means the gap between conditions is

over the early trials, which means that condition 1 participants are improving faster relative to condition 3. A null or negative coefficient would mean the conditions stay equally separated or diverge further.
